title: Resuscitation of the Young Infant (Chapter 4)
learningObjectives: Demonstrate the resuscitation of a sick young infant.

sections.title: Managing Airway and Breathing
sections.description: The letters A and B in ABCCCD represent airway and breathing. The clinician must assess airway patency, breathing effort, central cyanosis and signs of severe respiratory distress.

subsections.title: A. Airway
details: Neck is slightly extended; head tilt‑chin‑lift maneuver is used.
Conscious infant: inspect mouth, remove secretions, let infant find a comfortable position, give oxygen, continue assessment.
Unconscious infant: open airway with slight head tilt and chin lift, inspect mouth, remove secretions, check chest rise, insert airway if needed.

subsections.title: Insertion of an Oropharyngeal (Guedel) Airway
steps: Select an appropriately sized airway (extends from angle of mouth to angle of jaw with convex side up).
Position the child to open the airway.
Insert the airway with the convex side up.
If the airway is not patent, try a different size or reposition.

subsections.title: B. Breathing
signsOfSevereRespiratoryDistress: Unable to feed due to distress
subsections.oxygenManagement: Give oxygen via catheter/prongs; if distress worsens or central cyanosis persists, add high‑flow oxygen (≈4 L/min) via face mask.

bagMaskVentilation.description: If spontaneous ventilation is inadequate, ventilate with a self‑inflating bag and mask.
tips: Use a neutral head position; avoid hyper‑extension.
Place padding under the shoulders to prevent occipital flexion.
After five effective breaths, check femoral pulse for 10 seconds; if absent, begin chest compressions.

subsections.title: C. Chest Compressions
subsections.description: Chest compressions are performed as in neonatal resuscitation: 15 compressions followed by 2 ventilation breaths per cycle.
subsections.note: Respiratory arrest (breathless but with a pulse) is often salvageable; a true cardiorespiratory arrest (no pulse) is rarely survivable.

sections.title: Algorithm: Emergency assessment and management of NYI airway and breathing
sections.note: Zoom in on the image for details of the algorithm.

sections.title: E 1 Suction Machine (Pump)
sections.description: A suction machine is life‑saving equipment often needed in resuscitation of the young infant. See the equipment chapter (E1_Suctionpump.html) for more information.
